title: Spending Less after (Seemingly) Bad News
abstract: Using high-frequency spending data, we show that household consumption displays excess sensitivity to salient macroeconomic news, even when the news is not real. When the announced local unemployment rate reaches a 12-month maximum, local news coverage of unemployment increases and local consumers reduce their discretionary spending by 1.5% relative to consumers in areas with the same macroeconomic conditions. Low-income households display greater excess sensitivity to salience. The decrease in spending is not later reversed. Households in treated areas act as if they are more financially constrained than those in untreated areas with the same fundamentals.

article.introduction:
Macroeconomic data announcements provide informationof consequence for consumers. In addition to supplying statistics that are useful in guiding planning decisions, these announcements can change the tone of economic discussions. Salient announcements in particular may shift consumer sentiment. In this paper, we show that salient news coverage plays an important role in determining household spending and personal finance decisions. Consumers respond to the salience of adverse macro announcements about their region by cutting back on discretionary spending even when the news is uninformative about local macroeconomic fundamentals. Consumers whose areas experience salient negative economic news act as if they are more financially constrained than those in areas that receive less salient reports, even though underlying economic conditions do not differ across these regions. Seemingly bad news contributes significantly to quick and sharp consumption drops.
  We use local unemployment announcements at the CBSA (core-based statistical area) level as a natural experiment to measure the response of consumption to salient news in high-frequency household spending data. Salient news is likely to be communicated to consumers by the media. It is well established that media attention has a causal impact on the behavior of investors (Tetlock (2007), Engelberg and Parsons (2011), Dougal et al. (2012)). We examine whether the media also influences the consumption spending and financing choices of consumers. We begin by showing that U.S. newspapers devote attention to local unemployment announcements, with substantially more coverage of unemployment on dates close to local unemployment releases. The announcement of a 12-month local unemployment maximum is a particularly salient event for local media that leads to especially high coverage. Indeed, we find that local news interest in unemployment peaks specifically at 12-month maximums. Prior research (George and Hwang (2004), Huddart, Lang, and Yetman (2009), Driessen, Lin, and Van Hemert (2013)) shows that 52-week stock price highs are highlighted by newspapers and attract investor attention and that trading volume surges when new highs are achieved. In the same manner, 12-month unemployment maximums may be expected to seize the interest of both the media and consumers.
  We study the impact of 12-month maximums and the accompanying local media spotlight on unemployment by comparing the spending of households in areas that reach a new local maximum to the spending of similar households living in areas with the same macro fundamentals that do not reach a new maximum. We show that an announcement of a 12-month maximum in the local unemployment rate leads to a 1.5% drop in discretionary spending in the two weeks after the announcement. Announcement of near-maximums have no effect on spending, while false maximums (announced maximums that are later revised to be nonmaximums) have the same impact on consumers as actual maximums. We use real-time measurement error in unemployment announcements to identify the true announcement effect. Together, these results provide evidence of an excessively sensitive response by consumers to salient economic news. Spending drops after 12-month maximums but not after maximums at other horizons. Our empirical strategy is similar to recent work by Chodorow-Reich, Coglianese, and Karabarbounis (2019) who study the macroeconomic effects of unemployment benefit extensions.
  An increase in the unemployment rate represents an increase in the uncertainty faced by households. Households face a higher probability of job loss. An unemployment spell can be thought of as the realization of household-specific income disaster with potentially long-lasting effects on earnings (see Guvenen et al. (2017)). Standard consumer theory predicts that consumers respond to an increase in uncertainty by immediately reducing spending and saving more instead (Kimball (1990), Ganong and Noel (2019)). As a result of precautionary savings, a perceived increase in uncertainty in a CBSA would lead to a local reduction in consumption. In addition, households may rationally revise their expectations of future earnings downward and reduce consumption accordingly, in line with the permanent income hypothesis, when a higher unemployment rate is announced.
  Ourempirical design allows us to rule out these alternative, nonsentiment-based explanations of our findings. First, consumers are not responding to the actual unemployment rate, but rather to the announced rate, and then only when the announcement is salient. Second, the change in spending is not driven by long-term trends in unemployment—in our main specification we control for the announced unemployment rate as well as for current and lagged changes in the announced rate, and we find that 12-month maximums have a significant effect in reducing discretionary expenditures. Third, we do not observe a pretrend of declining spending before a 12-month maximum is announced—consumers sharply reduce their spending after the announcements themselves and do not appear to be responding to any general deterioration in economic conditions, and spending decreases quickly (within four days) after the announcement. Fourth, we show that 12-month maximum unemployment announcements are not associated with reduced future income for local consumers.
  The announcements also affect financing behavior. We find that a 12-month maximum announcement leads to a 1.4% decrease in credit card repayments. While consumers appear to be drawing down their existing credit lines, we find that they are less likely to initiate new loans. Salient negative unemployment announcements lead consumers to behave as if the financing environment has quickly become more challenging. Consumers also reduce their cash withdrawals, as another mechanism to maintain higher balances in their bank accounts. In sum, consumers in the treated areas act as if they are more financially constrained than those in the control areas, when in fact they are not.
  Our findings shed new light on previous studies that find using regional consumption data that the marginal propensity to consume out of wealth is larger for poorer households/areas (see Mian, Rao, and Sufi (2013) for a recent example). Using household-level spending data, we find that the consumption response to the salience of macro news varies across households. The resulting decline in household consumption is significantly larger for households with lower income. The heterogeneity in excess sensitivity that we describe, however, is a behavioral response that cannot be attributed to binding liquidity or solvency constraints or to news about fundamentals. Using high-frequency consumption data, Baugh et al. (2021) also find evidence that some households may act as if they are liquidity constrained when they are not.1
  Our findings are most convincingly explained by theories of consumer inattention. If consumers were always attentive, salience would not have any effect on their actual spending. A wealth of empirical evidence, however, suggests that investors and consumers do not pay attention to all information relevant to their optimization problem (see DellaVigna (2009) for an overview). For example, investors do not constantly monitor their portfolios (Brunnermeier and Nagel (2008)). There are two inattention-related interpretations of our findings. In the first, supply-driven, interpretation, inattentive households respond to salient macro announcements because they learn about the changes in macroeconomic conditions from local media coverage, which responds in turn to salience. Households can rationally choose to be inattentive to some information when they face constraints on information processing (Gabaix and Laibson (2001), Sims (2003), Reis (2006), Van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp (2009)). Consistent with this supply-side hypothesis, we find that the spending decrease after a 12-month maximum unemployment announcement is stronger in areas in which a greater fraction of households subscribe to local papers. The effect is also more pronounced when there is a surge in local media coverage of unemployment after the announcement. These results suggest that consumers are sensitive to the intensity of local media coverage.
  In the second, more behavioral, interpretation, consumers are hardwired to respond more to salient news. Selectively inattentive households choose when they pay attention; their reactions are not driven by the volume of news but rather by its salience. The large response to salient adverse news, especially by low-income households, is consistent with reference-dependent utility models (Kőszegi and Rabin (2006), Kőszegi and Rabin (2007,2009), Pagel (2017)).2As Pagel (2017) shows, consumers with reference-dependent utility are less attentive in response to fundamental negative aggregate shocks. Furthermore, low-income households are generally less attentive than high-income households because of the concavity of utility. Consistent with this demand-side hypothesis, we find stronger evidence of salience sensitivity for low-income households and for households that are less financially sophisticated.
  To the extent that salient news is more likely to spread, social networks may play a special role in propagating salient news within a CBSA (see Bailey et al. (2018a,2018b) for evidence on social networks and household spending). Shiller (2019) uses historical examples to show how economic stories can propagate like a virus. He refers to this analysis as narrative economics.
  Our paper is among the first to provide causal evidence of a quantitatively meaningful media-driven sentiment effect of nonfundamental news on consumption growth. In the time series, measures of consumer confidence and consumption growth are strongly correlated contemporaneously. Furthermore, there is some empirical evidence that measures of consumer confidence predict future consumption growth (Carroll, Fuhrer, and Wilcox (1994), Ludvigson (2004)). One explanation is that consumer confidence simply reflects households' expectations of future fundamentals. Another explanation is that changes in consumer confidence lead to changes in consumption growth. Ludvigson (2004) concludes that these measures have little incremental forecasting power for consumer spending beyond traditional macro and financial indicators, but Carroll, Fuhrer, and Wilcox (1994) argue that there is a pure sentiment effect in consumption, that is, that declines in sentiment causally lead to declines in consumption growth. Calibrated macro models suggest that consumer expectations and sentiment can explain a broad set of economic outcomes (Angeletos and La'O (2013), Beaudry and Portier (2014)) and that noise-induced changes in expectations may have an especially important role in driving short-term variation in consumption (Blanchard, L'Huillier, and Lorenzoni (2013)). Our work provides microlevel context for these claims by proposing specific sentiment shocks and showing that they have a causal impact on consumption.
  It is well understood that macro news is important for financial markets (Andersen et al. (2003), Evans and Lyons (2008), Bernile, Hu, and Tang (2016)). Our results show that individual consumers' spending and personal financing decisions are also influenced by macro releases. The results further show that consumer decisions are affected by news reporting. In particular, the results show that consumer confidence, a key determinant in many economic models, is somewhat fragile and subject to media persuasion.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the consumption and news data that we analyze. SectionIIdiscusses our empirical specification. SectionIIIpresents our results. SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Data:
We study data on the spending of U.S. consumers drawn from information on credit card and bank account transactions compiled by an online account aggregator. We build on recent work using high-frequency consumption data by Gelman et al. (2014), Olafsson and Pagel (2017,2018), and Baugh et al. (2021). The data cover 9,664,860 unique consumers drawn from all 50 U.S. states and the District of Columbia over the period January 2011 to December 2016. Figure1depicts the number of consumers in the sample across CBSAs.
  We observe transaction data from 24,827,483 bank accounts and 13,720,261 credit card accounts. Consumers hold a mean of 3.99 bank and credit card accounts. The aggregator classifies transactions into different spending categories. Our main interest is in studying discretionary spending. TableIlists the spending categories that we include in discretionary spending. The transactions are classified into granular spending subcategories by the data provider. We include in discretionary spending all of those spending subcategories that correspond to industries in the Consumer Discretionary sector according to the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS code 25).
  As we discuss in more detail below, we analyze the impact of unemployment announcements on consumer spending, and we treat the three days around the announcement as the announcement interval. We measure spending in two-week periods before and after announcement intervals. TableII, Panel A presents summary statistics for different spending categories. The data are aggregated by consumer at the two-week-period level. The mean discretionary spending per period is $329.1, of which roughly one-third is comprised of restaurant expenses. Average credit card repayments are $1,129.1. We find that a new loan is initiated by a consumer with a frequency of 0.5%. With respect to cash withdrawals, the average cash withdrawal per period is $405.3, and the average number of cash withdrawals is 2.4.
  TableII, Panel B provides demographic information about the consumers in the data. The median monthly salary per consumer that we observe is $2,857, which is similar to the $2,809 median monthly personal income in the 2018 data from the Current Population Survey, although our data do not cover all forms of personal income. Consumers in our data reside in zip codes with a 2010 median home value of $231,300 and a median educational attainment between some college and an associate's degree. Data from the 2010 American Community Survey display a median home value of $179,900, and the median educational attainment in the 2010 Census is some college. The consumers in our data may be wealthier to a moderate degree than the typical U.S. consumer, but they are broadly similar.
  In order to analyze the dissemination of information about local unemployment announcements, we consider data on news articles archived on NewsBank (newsbank.com). NewsBank includes records from a wide variety of national and local media sources in the United States.
article.II. Empirical Specification:
Our aim is to analyze the impact of local unemployment announcements on consumer spending. Our focus is on announcements that are 12-month maximums, as these announcements are likely to be particularly salient for consumers.3The local unemployment announcements we study are the Metropolitan Area Employment and Unemployment monthly news releases published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). These are distinct from the national-level monthly Employment Situation summaries that are also published by the BLS. The Employment Situation summaries for a given month tend to be released within roughly a week of the close of that month, while the Metropolitan Area Employment and Unemployment releases are usually published about one month after the month-end. The release dates for the national and local unemployment announcements therefore do not generally coincide.
  To study the impact of local unemployment announcements on consumer spending, we make three changes to the news regression specification. First, we treat the announcement date and subsequent two days as the announcement interval, and we omit this interval from our spending analysis. This allows consumers three days to absorb the unemployment announcements and to initiate any possible shifts in spending. Second, we measure spending decisions over two-week periods, as consumers do not make spending decisions over a daily horizon (and spending is not typically posted to bank and credit card accounts immediately in any case). Third, because our consumption data are at the individual consumer level, not simply the CBSA level, we compare the spending of each consumer before and after the announcement. The two-week spending period length avoids any overlap between the measured postannouncement spending of one month and the preannouncement spending of the subsequent month.
  We define event windowt$t$to include the two-week period immediately preceding an announcement, the announcement interval, and the two-week period immediately following the announcement. Event windows are thus roughly one month in duration. We measure each consumer's spending separately in the periods preceding and following each announcement interval. Figure2provides a timeline of the periods just described.
  In specification (4), the coefficient of interest isα′$\alpha ^{\prime }$, which measures the impact of a consumer-level characteristic on the spending response to a 12-month maximum. Theξi′$\xi _{i}^{\prime }$andθt′$\theta ^{\prime }_t$are fixed effects at the consumer and event window levels, respectively.
article.III. Results:
The empirical specification we propose in SectionIIconsiders the impact of a local unemployment announcement on news interest and consumer spending. We begin by providing evidence that news outlets offer additional coverage of unemployment in the days following an announcement; that is, the local news supply responds to the salience of the macro announcements.
  Specifically, as described in equation (1), we regressNational News Intereston an indicator for whether a day is a local unemployment release date along with fixed effects for the month-year and day of the week. As column (1) of TableIII, Panel A shows, release dates are associated with higherNational News Interest(coefficient = 0.051 andt$t$-statistic = 3.04). Thet$t$-statistic is clustered at the month-year level. This result indicates that national news interest in unemployment is approximately 5.1 percentage points higher on local unemployment announcement release dates (National News Interestlies on the range (0,1]). The result in column (2) of Panel A shows that interest is also higher, by approximately 2.8 percentage points (t$t$-statistic = 2.11), in the period consisting of the release date and the subsequent three days. As column (3) of Panel A shows, interest is still elevated in the week of the release date (coefficient = 0.028 andt$t$-statistic = 2.44). The level of news interest is higher on the announcement date than in the subsequent three to six days.
  The results in Panel A of TableIIIestablish that, in general, local unemployment release dates are associated with higher news interest in unemployment. The mechanism in SectionII, however, focuses on the impact of 12-month maximum unemployment rate announcements. We study the effect of these maximums on consumers by measuringLocal News Interestin “unemployment” and relating it to local unemployment announcements. This analysis requires information on the local unemployment rate released at the time (not the revised rate). Data for these vintages are provided for the period beginning May 2007 on the St. Louis ALFRED website.4We regressLocal News Intereston an indicator for whether the last released local unemployment rate was a 12-month maximum along with fixed effects for the day-month-year and CBSA, as described in equation (2).
  We find thatLocal News Interestin unemployment is significantly higher in CBSAs for which the last local unemployment rate announced was a 12-month maximum (coefficient = 0.0135,t$t$-statistic = 3.32), as displayed in column (1) of TableIII, Panel B. Thet$t$-statistic is double-clustered at the CBSA and month-year levels. The coefficient indicates that the consumer interest in unemployment is approximately 1.4 percentage points higher in these CBSAs. The inclusion of day-month-year fixed effects removes the influence of any national changes in unemployment rates; we are describing an increase in local news interest in unemployment after a local 12-month maximum. For a point of contrast, we define near-12-month-maximums as rates that are within 0.2 percentage points of a maximum but that are not maximums. Column (2) of Panel B shows that near-12-month maximums do not have a significant effect onLocal News Interest(coefficient = 0.0016,t$t$-statistic = 0.53). Relative to near-maximum announcements of high unemployment rates, the impact of a maximum is marked.
  The results in Panel B of TableIIIestablish that the number of media articles referencing “unemployment” is higher in CBSAs that experience 12-month maximums. We also find that these articles are longer. We collect word count data for all media records in NewsBank that mention “unemployment” for each CBSA-day-month-year that experiences a 12-month maximum. The data are gathered two days after local unemployment announcements are released. We also collect the same data for a randomly selected matching CBSA on the same day-month-year that does not experience a 12-month maximum. As detailed in TableIA.Iin theInternet Appendix, unemployment-mentioning articles have substantially higher word counts in CBSAs that have 12-month maximums.5Consumers in these areas are therefore exposed to both more and lengthier media discussions of unemployment.
  Is there something singular about 12-month maximums as opposed to maximums measured at different horizons? Every maximum at a horizon longer than 12 months is, of course, a 12-month maximum as well. To identify the incremental effect of a 12-month maximum announcement in particular, we consider announcements that are 12-month maximums but that are not 13-month maximums. This analysis enables us to distinguish 12-month announcement effects from effects associated with longer-horizon maximum announcements. In column (1) of TableIII, Panel C, we show thatLocal News Interestis significantly higher (coefficient = 0.0150,t$t$-statistic = 3.04) after 12-month maximum announcements that are not 13-month maximums. In fact, this coefficient is quite similar to the 1.4 percentage point effect generated by all 12-month maximums (as displayed in Panel B), suggesting that the impact of 12-month maximums arises from their achieving a maximum specifically at a 12-month horizon rather than at a longer horizon.
  We provide further evidence on this point by regressingLocal News Intereston 10-month maximums that are not 11-month maximums and analogously defined variables through to 14-month maximums that are not 15-month maximums. As shown in column (2) of Panel C, 12-month maximums that are not 13-month maximums have a significant effect (coefficient = 0.0152,t$t$-statistic = 3.05) while all other variables have insignificant (and smaller) coefficients. Twelve-month maximums therefore appear to capture the imagination of the news media, leading to increased coverage in ways that maximums at other horizons do not. As we turn to the consumer spending analysis, we therefore focus our attention on 12-month maximums.6
  These results show that the supply of news is sensitive to the salience of the macro announcement. This is intuitive, given that newspapers are known to cater to the tastes of their readership (Gentzkow and Shapiro (2010)). Presumably, consumers have a taste for salient news. As shown next, this slant in news coverage impacts household spending decisions.
  Local unemployment announcements attract the attention of the news. Do they impact spending? We address this question by measuring discretionary spending for each consumer in the two-week periods before and after local unemployment announcement periods and assessing the impact of maximum announcements on the observed change in spending. Specifically, we estimate equation (3), where we regress the change in the log of one plus discretionary spending on an indicator for a 12-month maximum announcement and fixed effects at the consumer and event-window levels. The inclusion of these fixed effects allows us to isolate the change in spending for a given consumer around a specific announcement. We measure unemployment rate announcements using the vintage data that specify the actual rate announced at the time.
  As can be seen in column (1) of TableIV, we find that a 12-month maximum announcement leads to a 1.5% drop in discretionary spending (thet$t$-statistic, which is double-clustered at the CBSA and time-period levels, is −3.97). This is a quick, sizable response to a maximum announcement. Within the space of two weeks, consumers undertake a meaningful reduction in their discretionary spending. Consumers are not simply responding to high unemployment rate announcements. When we include fixed effects for the announced unemployment rate as controls, the estimated effect is little changed (coefficient = −1.4% andt$t$-statistic = −3.54), as shown in column (2) of TableIV.7
  It may be argued that perhaps consumers' responses to local unemployment rate 12-month maximum announcements are a reaction to underlying economic changes and not to the releases themselves. It may be the case, for example, that local economic conditions materially deteriorate in advance of a 12-month maximum announcement. Consumers who notice this negative economic trend may engage in an immediate shift in their current spending patterns that is unrelated to any BLS news release. To evaluate this argument, we include controls for the current change in the announced unemployment rate and each of the lagged changes over the prior three announcements. As displayed in column (3) of TableIV, none of these announced unemployment rate changes significantly impacts the change in spending, and the coefficient on 12-month maximums is little altered by their inclusion in the regression. In column (4) of TableIV, we report the results from our most comprehensive specification that includes fixed effects at the level of one-tenth of a percentage point for the unemployment rate, the current change, and each of the three lagged changes. The estimated coefficient is −1.7% with at$t$-statistic of −4.89. Across all of the specifications, with or without controls, we find highly consistent results, which indicate that medium-term unemployment changes are not driving our results.
  Our results clearly show that consumers reduce their discretionary spending after 12-month maximum unemployment announcements. Consumers are most likely to learn of these announcements from media sources and, as we show in TableIII, local newspapers devote high levels of coverage to unemployment after these releases. Our findings are therefore consistent with a causal effect of local media on consumer decisions that is analogous to that documented for investors (Tetlock (2007), Engelberg and Parsons (2011), Dougal et al. (2012)).
  As an additional test, we consider the impact of near-12-month maximums. TableIIIshows that near-maximums, despite being high announced rates, do not garner disproportionate media interest. If consumers are responding to salient unemployment news conveyed by the media, then we would not expect them to change their spending much after a near-12-month maximum announcement. The result in column (1) of TableVis consistent with this view: we find an insignificant effect (coefficient = −0.3% andt$t$-statistic = −0.77) of near-maximums on the change in spending. The inclusion of near-maximums in the regression has little effect on the estimated effect of actual announced 12-month maximums (coefficient = −1.5% andt$t$-statistic = −4.02). Near-maximums also have an insignificant impact on the change in spending in a specification that includes unemployment rate and change fixed effects, as shown in column (2) of TableV. Twelve-month maximums apparently have salience for consumers that distinguishes them from near-maximums.
  Research on investor responses to 52-week stock price highs (George and Hwang (2004), Driessen, Lin, and Van Hemert (2013)) finds evidence consistent with theories of both investor attention (Barber and Odean (2008)) and anchoring. Investors focus their attention on 52-week highs and price volatility increases when these highs are breached. Our results showing the importance of 12-month maximum announcements are also well explained by the argument that these releases attract the attention of newspapers and consumers. The trading literature further shows that as shares approach 52-week maximums, volatility declines and news is incorporated into prices more slowly. These results are interpreted as evidence of an anchoring effect whereby investors view the 52-week high as akin to a resistance boundary that induces different investor behavior at prices just below the maximum. In our setting, the analogous result would be a shift in spending patterns at near-maximums when controlling for the level of the unemployment rate. If consumers view the 12-month maximum as a resistance boundary, then they should change their spending at near-maximums as they approach 12-month highs. We do not find this result in TableV, so our findings are better explained by attention theories than by anchoring.
  To assess the pure announcement effects of local unemployment releases (as opposed to a response to underlying economic changes), we analyze false 12-month maximums. These are local unemployment announcements that are 12-month maximums at the time of announcement but that are subsequently revised and that, after revision, are revealed to have not actually been a maximum. Chodorow-Reich, Coglianese, and Karabarbounis (2019) use real-time measurement error in unemployment announcements to determine the macroeconomic effects of unemployment benefit extensions. We use the same measurement error to isolate the true 12-month maximum announcement effect.
  In column (3) of TableV, we find that false 12-month maximums reduce discretionary spending (coefficient = −1.8% andt$t$-statistic = −5.25) in approximately the same manner as actual 12-month maximums. Consumers appear quite clearly to be responding to the announced unemployment rates. A similar result emerges in the specification with unemployment rate and change fixed effects, as shown in column (4) of TableV. The final two columns show that false and true maximums have analogous effects; the coefficients are statistically indistinguishable (p≥0.51$p \ge 0.51$).
  Chodorow-Reich, Coglianese, and Karabarbounis (2019) describe the use by the BLS of a state-space filter to estimate the unemployment rate. Revisions arise due to improved statistical methodologies, the arrival of more complete data, and the utilization at the time of the revision of the full time series of data. The error in the BLS procedure may therefore not be orthogonal to actual conditions, and hence there is a concern that the response of consumers to false maximums may be driven by other economic changes. We argue that this phenomenon is unlikely to be generating our results for two reasons. First, Chodorow-Reich, Coglianese, and Karabarbounis (2019) show that improved statistical methodologies have by far the largest impact on revisions, while updated data have minor effects. One important set of changes reduced the excessive cross-state spillover impact of outliers in the previous BLS model. The implementation of these technical improvements that led to revisions was not caused by variation in the true economic circumstances in a local area.
  Second, the revisions provide more accurate estimates of the correct unemployment rate. The revisions thus indicate that there was an error in the original announcement that was correlated with local economic conditions, but the error was that the initial rate was overstated. In other words, by focusing on false maximums, we are examining cases in which the error was that economic conditions were better than the announcement indicated. In actuality, the true unemployment rate at false maximums was often a near-maximum, yet we show in TableVthat spending drops after false maximum announcements and not after near-maximums. That the spending decreases are very similar for all maximums and for false maximums provides clear evidence that consumers are responding to the announcement of the maximum.
  As displayed in Figure3, 12-month maximums occur throughout the six-year period over which we have spending data. There is seasonality in the timing of these maximums—they are clustered in the January to March and June to August periods. The tests in TablesIVandV, however, include fixed effects at the event-window level, where event windows are approximately one month in length. All of our spending change comparisons, therefore, are across consumers in a given month. Any seasonal differences in within-month changes in spending are absorbed by the fixed effects and do not influence the estimated coefficients.8
  TableIIIshows that news interest is heightened after 12-month maximums that are not 13-month maximums and that this effect does not obtain for similar maximums at other horizons. We next assess whether consumers also respond differently to 12-month maximums by regressing the change in discretionary spending on an indicator for 12-month maximums that are not 13-month maximums and the standard controls. We find, as shown in column (1) of TableVI, Panel A, that consumers spend significantly less (coefficient = −1.3% andt$t$-statistic = −2.30) after 12-month maximums that are not 13-month maximums. As for news interest, we find that the impact on consumer spending of a 12-month maximum is quite similar in magnitude to the impact of a 12-month but not 13-month maximum. In column (2) of TableVI, Panel A, we show that near-12-month maximums have an insignificant effect on changes in spending and have almost no impact on the estimated effect of 12-month but not 13-month maximums. The results displayed in column (3) of Panel A show that 10-month but not 11-month maximums through 14-month but not 15-month maximums all have insignificant effects on consumer spending with the exception that the impact of 12-month but not 13-month maximums is significant (coefficient = −1.2% andt$t$-statistic = −2.12).
  We find similar results for false maximums. In Panel B of TableVI, we show that 12-month but not 13-month maximums at announcement that were later revised and revealed not to have been 12-month maximums have a negative impact on consumer spending (coefficient = −1.6% andt$t$-statistic = −2.61). The other false maximums of this form have insignificant effects at horizons ranging from 10 to 14 months. The distinctive response that we find for 12-month maximums in particular suggests that the false maximum effect is unlikely to be driven by a correlation with unobserved economic variables, as there is no reason to expect that correlation to hold at only a 12-month horizon.9The news media highlight 12-month maximums in particular and consumer spending drops for maximums at a 12-month horizon.10
  In our main analysis, we consider discretionary expenditures within two-week periods, which allows us to measure changes in spending around monthly unemployment announcements without overlap across observations. A two-week spending period is also a reasonable interval given prior work suggesting that for discretionary-type goods, consumers make just over two in-store shopping trips per month and have an online buying frequency of approximately six to nine occasions per month (Farag, Schwanen, and Dijst (2005), Ding and Lu (2017), Shi et al. (2021)). Consistent with these results, we find that consumers engage in discretionary transactions on a median of five (mean = 5.55) days per two-week period. Nonetheless, to more precisely identify the timing of the impact of local unemployment announcements on consumption and to investigate any pretrends, we analyze spending at the shorter span of weekly data. Within each event window, we label as Weeks 1 and 2 the two weeks before the announcement interval and as Weeks 3 and 4 the two weeks following the announcement. Taking Week 2 as the reference week, we regress the change in discretionary spending in each other week relative to Week 2 on an indicator for whether a 12-month maximum was announced during the window and the full set of controls including consumer, event window, unemployment rate, and unemployment change fixed effects. TableIVshows that 12-month maximum announcements lead to a subsequent discretionary spending decline. If this spending decline does not reflect a response to the announcement but instead reflects consumers' general responses to their personal assessments of declining economic conditions, then a soon-to-be-released 12-month maximum announcement should be associated with a pretrend of declining spending from Week 1 to Week 2. If consumers are instead reacting specifically to the local unemployment maximum, then there should be no pretrend and discretionary spending should drop from Week 2 to Week 3 after these announcements.
  We present the results comparing discretionary spending in Week 1 to Week 2 in column (1) of Panel A of TableVII. As can be seen in the table, we find that the 12-month maximum indicator is negative and insignificant (coefficient = −0.6% andt$t$-statistic = −0.71). Had there been a pretrend of decreasing spending, a positive and significant coefficient would have been observed (i.e., there would have been higher spending in Week 1), which is clearly not the case. Hence, there is no evidence of a decline in spending before the announcement. We do, however, find a negative and significant coefficient on the 12-month maximum indicator when comparing spending in Week 4 to Week 2 (coefficient = −2.2% andt$t$-statistic = −4.36), as displayed in column (2) of Panel A, which shows an immediate negative discretionary spending response after the announcement. We also find, as detailed in column (3) of Panel A, a negative and significant effect of 12-month maximums on the change in spending between Week 4 to Week 2 (coefficient = −2.8% andt$t$-statistic = −2.84), indicating a sustained decrease in spending over a period of two weeks. These results support the argument that discretionary spending falls after a 12-month maximum announcement due to the announcement itself and not due to a continuing pretrend of declining spending. The coefficient estimates are depicted in Figure4.
  We show in columns (4) to (6) of TableVII, Panel A that near-12-month maximum announcements display neither pretrends nor subsequent spending declines. In Panel B of TableVII, we report results showing that 12-month false maximums are not preceded by spending declines but are followed by significant decreases in discretionary spending. The weekly results are consistent with the argument that 12-month maximum announcements have a causal effect in reducing local discretionary spending.
  As discussed above, spending does not occur at a very high frequency, but as an additional test we analyze the change in spending in the four-day period after the announcement interval relative to the four-day period just before. We find a 3.7% (t$t$-statistic = −3.44) drop in discretionary spending after a 12-month maximum announcement, as displayed in column (1) of TableVIII. This quick, sharp drop in spending right around the announcement offers clear evidence that discretionary expenditures respond to the announcement rather than to long- or medium-term economic changes. There is no evidence at this time interval of a response to near-12-month maximums, but there is a drop in spending after false 12-month maximums, as shown in columns (2) and (3) of TableVIII.11Taking the weekly and four-day-period results together, we find that there is no pretrend of a spending decline before 12-month maximum unemployment releases while there is a rapid decrease in spending after the announcements.
  The results in TablesIVtoVIIIuse an event-study methodology to demonstrate the quick response of consumers' discretionary spending to local unemployment announcements. We now consider the duration of these responses. Does an unemployment maximum depress spending for a couple of weeks only to lead to a subsequent reversal? Or, by contrast, do these announcements have lingering effects that have a negative impact on spending in future periods?
  Addressing these questions requires that we examine medium-term effects of announcements, so we can no longer focus only on shifts in spending in a short window around a release date. Instead, we examine the impact of a 12-month unemployment maximum announcement on changes in expenditures over periods ranging from the next month to the next six months; the empirical specification is detailed in theInternet Appendix. In TableIA.VI, we show that the lagged 12-month maximum announcement has an insignificant negative effect on future spending at all of these leads. There is no evidence of a positive response at any horizon. Changes in discretionary spending prompted by local 12-month maximum unemployment releases materialize within two weeks of the announcements, as shown in TablesIVandVII, and spending returns to normal levels in the medium term. There is clearly no evidence of later reversals in spending in which postannouncement decreases are following by periods of above-average expenditures.
  In general, our findings on spending are consistent with the causal effects of media on stock markets described by Tetlock (2007) and Dougal et al. (2012). As a point of contrast, however, those papers show that media effects on markets are often reversed after a period of time, while we do not find reversals in TableIA.VI. In stock markets, a large number of other traders exist who can undo sentiment-driven price changes. There is no similar mechanism that forces individual consumers to reverse their sentiment-motivated changes in spending patterns.
  Ganong and Noel (2019) find that households dramatically cut spending when they lose their jobs. We argue that our results are not driven by an increase in the local unemployment rate being associated with consumers in our panel losing their jobs and therefore spending less, for three reasons. First, as we show in the timeline in Figure2, the announcement describes job losses that occurred roughly one to two months earlier while the spending declines detailed in TablesIV,VII, andVIIItake place in the two-week, one-week, and four-day periods, respectively, surrounding the announcement. Second, if higher job losses in the previous one to two months led to a decline in subsequent spending, then a pretrend of reduced spending should be observed before the current announcement. TableVIIshows, however, there is no evidence of such a pretrend. Spending declines only after the announcement itself. Third, higher job losses in the preperiod should lead to announced maximums at various horizons, but TableVIshows that consumption declines after 12-month maximums specifically and not at maximums of other horizons.
  There is also no evidence that 12-month maximum unemployment announcements are associated with reduced future income for local consumers. In TableIA.VII, we show that at horizons of one to six months, current 12-month maximums do not predict a reduction in future income. The spending decline after a 12-month maximum announcement appears to be driven by news and/or behavioral factors rather than by the timing of job losses or income reduction for the consumers in our panel. Our results may be distinguished from those of Stephens (2004), who finds that workers who largely correctly anticipate future job losses do not appear to engage in consumption smoothing to reduce the impact of their expected employment terminations. We show that a media-induced negative sentiment shock does lead to reduced spending, even though it is unrelated to future income changes. In both settings, consumers appear to divert from careful consumption smoothing, though for different reasons. It may be the case that marked changes in perceived circumstances are more likely to attract the attention of consumers than slow evolutions. Consumption decreases would thus follow actual job losses or sharp changes in sentiment rather than gradual changes in beliefs about the tenure of future employment.
  The analysis in TableIVdescribes the impact of local unemployment announcements on discretionary spending as a whole. In this section, we use the same two-week-post versus two-week-prior event-study method outlined in equation (3) to describe the effects of these announcements on some of the specific categories of discretionary spending to provide a richer understanding of the phenomenon. De Nardi, French, and Benson (2012) emphasize that nondurables and services declined most dramatically during the Great Recession. As can be seen in Figure5, we observe a negative effect on spending in each of the 17 discretionary categories. (These category-level regressions only include observations with at least some spending in that category in either the pre- or postperiod around the unemployment announcement interval.) The negative impact is significant at the 5% level for seven categories and at the 10% level for 10 categories. The strongest effects are observed for auto expenses, restaurant spending, gifts, and entertainment. Most of these categories have the flavor of luxury spending for many consumers. The weakest effects are for child expenses, charitable giving, and clothing.
  TableIVshows that unemployment maximums reduce spending. We now consider the impact of these releases on consumers' financing strategies. Consumers whose future expectations have been dimmed by the announcement of a local unemployment maximum may choose to preserve some financial slack to be able to respond to worsening conditions. This can be regarded as a form of precautionary saving (Kimball (1990)). One strategy for creating such slack is to reduce debt repayments and effectively keep more cash on hand. We investigate whether consumers react in this way by regressing the change in the log of one plus credit card repayments on an indicator for a 12-month maximum announcement and the standard controls, as described in equation (3). Only event windows in which some credit card repayment is observed are included. In column (1) of TableIX, Panel A, we find that consumers reduce their credit card repayments by 1.4% (t$t$-statistic = −3.89).
  Credit card repayments are typically made at a lag to the actual purchases, as it is standard for issuers to offer a “grace period” of three weeks or longer during which interest does not accrue if the balance is eventually fully paid (Ausubel (1991)). Thus, our finding that consumers reduce their credit card repayments likely does not reflect decreased spending (like that documented in TablesIVtoVIII), as the balance due on a credit card typically reflects expenditures from an earlier period. In other words, consumers face a credit card balance that was accumulated largely before the local unemployment announcement. Their reduced repayments therefore describe a financing response to the unemployment release rather than a consumption response.
  A rational precautionary savings strategy should respond similarly to high unemployment announcements whether they are 12-month maximums or near-maximums, but as we demonstrate in column (2) of TableIX, Panel A, we find no impact of near-maximums on credit card repayments (coefficient = −0.3% andt$t$-statistic = −1.12). Furthermore, in column (3) of Panel A we show that false 12-month maximums have a comparable negative effect (coefficient = −1.6% andt$t$-statistic = −2.97) on repayments to that of actual maximums.
  Taken together, these results clearly demonstrate that maximum announcements have salience for consumers that leads to a substantial change in financing behavior that may be difficult to completely rationalize. Beyond the notable differences in the consumer responses to (false) maximums and near-maximums, credit card interest rates are very high. Consumers who react to a 12-month maximum by repaying less and therefore borrowing more on their credit cards are acquiring financial slack at a rather high cost.
  Do consumers also respond to unemployment maximums by seeking new financing? In column (1) of TableIX, Panel B, we show that this is not the case: a 12-month maximum announcement leads to a reduced probability of initiating a loan (coefficient = −0.05% andt$t$-statistic = −3.23). This is a large effect, given that the mean frequency of initiating a loan is 0.5%. Near-maximums do not decrease the likelihood of starting a new loan (coefficient = 0.03% andt$t$-statistic = 0.87), while false maximums do reduce the probability of loan initiation (coefficient = −0.03% andt$t$-statistic = −2.15), as shown in columns (2) and (3) of Panel B, respectively.
  The results in TableIXshow that unemployment maximum announcements lead consumers to draw down more of their current lines of credit by repaying less of their credit card debt, but also reduce the probability that they obtain new financing, perhaps because they are less likely to seek a new loan. Consumers may view unemployment maximums as harbingers of worsening economic conditions, in which case it is prudent to maintain cash by borrowing against existing credit commitments but one is unlikely to be granted additional debt. An announced unemployment maximum (whether actual or false) leads consumers to act as if they are more financially constrained, while near-maximums have no such effect.
  Consumers also respond to unemployment 12-month maximums by withdrawing 1.5% less (t$t$-statistic = −2.94) cash from their bank accounts, as we show in column (1) of TableX, Panel A. Smaller cash withdrawals may reflect lower current spending or a plan to spend less in the future. TableIVdocuments a roughly 1.5% reduction in credit or debit card-based spending. We find a similar effect here on cash disbursement, which is likely tightly tied to cash spending. The reduction in the cash withdrawn is driven by maximums and not near-maximums (column (2) of Panel A), and false maximums have a similar impact to that of maximums (column (3) of Panel A). In Panel B of TableX, we present regressions in which the dependent variable is the log of the number of cash withdrawals and describe results that are similar though somewhat weaker than those for the amount of withdrawals.
  The findings in TablesIXandXshow that consumers respond to local unemployment maximum announcements by maintaining higher balances in their bank accounts. They do so by reducing credit card repayments and by withdrawing less cash. Both of these policies increase a consumer's short-term financial flexibility, though the credit card repayment strategy potentially comes at a considerable price.
  In TableIV, we demonstrate that consumers reduce their discretionary spending in response to announcements of local unemployment maximums. In this section, we analyze the heterogeneous responses of different groups of consumers.
  There are conflicting arguments as to whether high-income consumers are more or less responsive to announcements of unemployment maximums. On the one hand, the consumption of people with higher incomes is likely to be more sensitive to stock market movements, so their consumption may also be more sensitive to unemployment releases that are another form of economic news. It is also probably the case that high-income consumers are more frequently exposed to information about the announced local unemployment rate.12On the other hand, one may argue that unemployment developments are more relevant to lower-income consumers and these consumers have a higher marginal propensity to consume (Mian, Rao, and Sufi (2013)), so they should respond more to unemployment information. Moreover, for lower-income consumers, discretionary spending is a smaller fraction of their overall expenditures, so their discretionary component may be more sensitive to negative news.
  We test these competing hypotheses by regressing the change in the log of one plus discretionary spending on an indicator for a local 12-month unemployment maximum, the interaction between this indicator and a consumer-level income characteristic, and the standard set of controls. In this specification, detailed in (4), we are interested in the coefficient on the interaction term that describes the differential response of consumers with the specified characteristic to the announcement of a local unemployment maximum.
  The first characteristic we study is an indicator for consumers with high salaries. We calculate the median average salary for all consumers and describe a consumer as having a high salary if her average salary is above this median. (This characteristic, and all those described below, are time-invariant at the consumer level.) We find, as described in column (1) of TableXI, Panel A, a positive and significant interaction for the high-salary characteristic (coefficient = 0.9% andt$t$-statistic = 2.47). This evidence suggests that high-salary consumers respond less to local unemployment maximums. The larger magnitude of the coefficient on the local maximum (coefficient = −1.9% andt$t$-statistic = −4.38) indicates that high-salary consumers do reduce their consumption after a maximum is announced, but they do not reduce it by as much as low-salary consumers do.
  As a second measure of consumer wealth, we define an indicator for whether the consumer has above-median security trading expenses (expressed as a fraction of total expenditures). We find that consumers with high security trading expenses are also less responsive to local 12-month unemployment maximums, as detailed in column (2) of Panel A. Our third proxy for consumer wealth is an indicator for whether the consumer resides in a zip code with a median house value in the 2010 census that is above the sample median. We find that consumers residing in high-value neighborhoods do not respond differently after 12-month maximums.
  The results in Panel A support the argument that high-income consumers reduce their discretionary spending following local unemployment maximums less aggressively than do low-income consumers. This result may be driven by the salience of unemployment news for low-income consumers or by their higher marginal propensity to consume (especially for discretionary spending).
  We next provide additional evidence on how a consumer's response to unemployment news is affected by the fraction of her spending that is fixed versus discretionary by considering high-utility-expenditure consumers, who are defined as those for whom utility expenses as a fraction of total expenditures are above the overall median. In column (1) of TableXI, Panel B, we find that high-utility-expenditure consumers respond more strongly to local 12-month unemployment maximums (interaction coefficient = −0.9% andt$t$-statistic = −3.03). Consumers with high telephone spending and high gas spending (in both cases relative to overall expenditures) also cut their discretionary spending more after 12-month maximums, as shown in columns (2) and (3) of Panel B. These results are directly consistent with a mechanism whereby a consumer with a larger fixed component of expenditures must reduce her discretionary spending more quickly after negative news, but they are not in tension with the argument that unemployment news is more salient for low-income consumers.
  Above we show that consumers respond to maximum unemployment announcements, even those that are subsequently revised, but not to near-maximums, and we argue that this set of responses may be difficult to rationalize. To the extent that is the case, consumers more subject to behavioral biases should respond more forcefully to these announcements. We test this hypothesis by defining high-education consumers as those who reside in zip codes that had an above-median average-weighted level of education in the 2010 census. These consumers may be less influenced by behavioral biases (so their response may be muted), but they may also be more likely to learn of local unemployment releases (which would suggest a greater response). In column (1) of TableXI, Panel C, we show that the response of high-education consumers to 12-month unemployment maximum announcements is not significantly different from that of lower-education consumers. Despite the fact that high-education consumers are likely to have higher incomes, they do not respond less to these announcements.
  As a second proxy for behavioral biases, we define high-service-charge consumers as those with above-median bank service charges as a fraction of their total expenditures. A consumer who spends a large amount on these fees, relative to her income, may make other less-optimal financial decisions as well. In column (2) of Panel C, we show that high-service-charge consumers do indeed react more strongly to unemployment maximum announcements.
  Our third characteristic is a measure of risk aversion. We label as high-insurance consumers those who spend an above-median fraction of their total expenditures on insurance. These consumers may be particularly concerned about any negative news and therefore respond more vigorously to a 12-month unemployment maximum. In column (3) of Panel C, we show that this is indeed the case—consumers who spend relatively more on insurance reduce their discretionary spending more after a local unemployment maximum announcement (interaction coefficient = −0.7% andt$t$-statistic = −2.11).
  The results in TableXIdemonstrate that while high-income consumers do respond to local unemployment maximum announcements, they do so in a more restrained manner than their lower-income peers. From a macroeconomic perspective, it is therefore the case that negative news about unemployment should have a larger impact on discretionary spending in lower-income regions and neighborhoods. Policy makers who are interested in mitigating these effects should therefore concentrate their attention on less favored areas.
  Our estimates of sentiment-based decreases in consumption should be viewed as aggregate impacts; the treatment effect on the treated consumers who receive the unemployment information is likely higher. We test this hypothesis by assessing the impact of news exposure on discretionary spending through two types of tests. In the first, we use data from the Alliance for Audited Media to find the coverage of local newspapers (defined as paid circulation per household) across different zip codes. Circulation data are provided for approximately 8,000 zip codes. We regress the change in the log of one plus discretionary spending on an indicator for a local 12-month unemployment maximum, the interaction between this indicator and the household coverage rate of the local newspaper with the highest coverage, and the standard set of controls. (Household coverage itself is absorbed by the consumer fixed effects.) As shown in column (1) of TableXII, Panel A, we find that the response to a 12-month maximum is stronger in areas with higher rates of local newspaper household coverage (coefficient = −0.040 andt$t$-statistic = −2.28). The results indicate that as local newspaper coverage varies from 0% to 100%, the discretionary spending response to a 12-month maximum shifts from −1.4% to −5.4%. These regression estimates make clear that increased exposure to local newspapers is associated with a stronger decrease in discretionary spending, but the treatment effect on consumers who are very likely to see a local newspaper is not much larger in an absolute sense than the effect on those without such access. How can there be a spending response in areas in which local newspaper coverage is 0%? Americans receive news from multiple sources, including television and radio, and the circulation data only reflect newspaper coverage: in 2016, 57% of U.S. adults often received news via television (with 46% often watching local television news), and 25% often received news via radio.13These survey results indicate that a substantial fraction of the population is likely to receive local news about unemployment in some format.
  As noted above, high-income households are more likely to read newspapers, and the results in TableXIshow that the consumers with high salaries respond less to 12-month maximums. It is therefore possible that the true impact of household newspaper coverage is stronger than is depicted in column (1) due to a potential confounding effect with income. To mitigate this concern, we include in the regression as a control the interaction between the above-median salary dummy and the 12-month maximum indicator. The effect of household newspaper coverage is little changed (coefficient = −0.044 andt$t$-statistic = −2.43), as can be seen in column (2) of TableXII, Panel A. Measuring the intensity of local newspaper coverage using the sum of household coverages for all local newspapers yields similar findings, as detailed in columns (3) and (4) of Panel A.
  In a second set of tests, we consider the effect of an increase in news coverage on the discretionary spending response. For each CBSA and month, we calculateChange in Local News InterestasLocal News Interestin the two weeks after the unemployment announcement period minusLocal News Interestin the two weeks before the announcement period (whereLocal News Interestis defined as in TableIII). We regress the change in discretionary spending on an indicator for the 12-month maximum, the interaction between this indicator andChange in Local News Interest, and the usual controls. As shown in column (1) of TableXII, Panel B, discretionary spending decreases more after a 12-month maximum in CBSAs that experience a surge in unemployment news coverage (coefficient = −0.052 andt$t$-statistic = −3.08). This is evidence that increased intensity of media attention on unemployment is associated with a sharper drop in discretionary spending. An admittedly unrealistic shift inLocal News Interestfrom the lowest rank in the preannouncement period to the highest rank in the postannouncement period would result in a 6.9% decrease in spending after a 12-month maximum, which may be compared to the 1.7% decrease in spending following a 12-month maximum associated with no change inLocal News Interest. Including the interaction between the high-salary dummy and the 12-month maximum indicator has little impact on the estimated effect, as shown in column (2) of Panel B.
  In sum, the results displayed in TableXIIoffer clear support for the view that consumers respond more strongly to unemployment news in areas with higher local newspaper household coverage and in cities in which existing media outlets devote greater attention to unemployment news.
article.IV. Conclusion:
Consumer sentiment is often argued to be an important factor driving of spending, but this hypothesis is difficult to test as it is challenging to identify sentiment shocks and to understand why they arise. We find that the announcement of a 12-month local unemployment maximum captures the attention of the press and leads to more local news about unemployment. Consumers are most likely to access unemployment announcement information via the media, and we show that 12-month maximums lead to a 1.5% reduction in consumers' discretionary spending. Near-maximum announcements that receive less media attention have no effect on spending, but false maximums that were later revised downward have the same impact as actual maximums. Announcements of 12-month maximums in particular have a negative effect on consumption that is not found at other horizons. Unemployment releases that are 12-month maximums also lead consumers to reduce their credit card repayments by 1.4% and to decrease their likelihood of initiating a new loan by 0.05%. These results suggest that salient negative macroeconomic announcements have a strong effect on the spending and personal finance decisions of consumers that may be mediated by their exposure to news. The reductions in discretionary spending are strongest for lower-income consumers. The decrease in spending is not followed by above-average expenditures in subsequent months. Effects are stronger in areas with higher intensity of local news coverage.
  Our results suggest that media attention is an important determinant of consumer confidence and therefore spending. A full understanding of this phenomenon would require a model in which the media act as a crucial bridge between the government's reporting of economic data and the absorption of this information by consumers. A theory of this kind must also analyze the incentives of various media to filter some reports and amplify others. Profit-seeking media outlets presumably seek out salient news that interests consumers. The nature of consumer inattention will thus help shape the organization and structure of media and will influence the information provided to all market participants.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
